Osmania University · University College of Engineering (A)

Engineering Mathematics
I – IV

The complete syllabus and a semester-by-semester study plan for the four mathematics papers in BE Biomedical Engineering — from sequences and calculus to transforms and numerical methods.

Programme: BE Biomedical Engineering Duration: 4 papers · Sem 1–4 Pattern: 70 SEE + 30 CIE
01

The four papers at a glance

Mathematics runs through the first two years. Papers I and II are common to all engineering branches; Paper III is the Biomedical Engineering–specific paper; Paper IV covers transforms, numerical methods and statistics.

CodePaperSemesterCreditsFocus
BS 101 MTMathematics – ISemester 13Sequences, calculus, vector calculus
BS 201 MTMathematics – IISemester 23Matrices, differential equations
BS 901 MTMathematics – IIISemester 33Complex variables, PDE, Fourier
BS 401 MTMathematics – IVSemester 43Transforms, numerical methods, statistics
Semester 1 · Basic Sciences

Mathematics – I

Code BS 101 MT 4 periods/wk (3T + 1 Tut) SEE 70 · CIE 30

Course objectives

  • Introduce sequences, series and their properties.
  • Build the tools of curve sketching.
  • Cover functions of several variables and multiple integrals.
  • Develop vector differential and integral calculus.

Course outcomes

  • Determine the nature (convergence) of sequences and series.
  • Sketch the graphs of given curves.
  • Use multiple integrals to find areas and volumes of bounded regions.
  • Apply these methods across the wider curriculum.
UNIT I

Sequences & Series

Sequences and series; general properties of series; series of positive terms; comparison tests; D'Alembert's ratio test; Raabe's test; Cauchy's root test; alternating series; series of positive and negative terms; absolute and conditional convergence.

UNIT II

Differential Calculus

Rolle's theorem; Lagrange's and Cauchy's mean value theorems; Taylor's series; curvature; circle of curvature; radius of curvature; centre of circle of curvature; envelope of a family of curves; asymptotes to a curve; curve sketching.

UNIT III

Functions of Several Real Variables

Functions of two variables; limits and continuity; partial derivatives; total differential and differentiability; derivatives of composite and implicit functions (chain rules); change of variables; Jacobian; higher-order partial derivatives; Taylor's series of two variables; maxima and minima; Lagrange's method of multipliers.

UNIT IV

Multiple Integrals

Double integrals; change of order of integration; triple integrals; change of variables in integrals; applications to areas and volumes.

UNIT V

Vector Calculus

Scalar and vector fields; gradient of a scalar field; directional derivative; divergence and curl of a vector field; line, surface and volume integrals; Green's theorem in a plane; Gauss's divergence theorem; Stoke's theorem (without proof).

Why it matters for BME — Vector calculus (divergence, gradient, curl) is the language of bioelectric fields and blood-flow modelling; multiple integrals underpin area/volume computations used later in imaging and biomechanics.

Suggested reading

  1. R. K. Jain & S. R. K. Iyengar, Advanced Engineering Mathematics, Narosa, 4th ed., 2014.
  2. B. S. Grewal, Higher Engineering Mathematics, Khanna, 43rd ed., 2014.
  3. Erwin Kreyszig, Advanced Engineering Mathematics, Wiley, 9th ed., 2012.
  4. G. B. Thomas, Weir & Hass, Thomas' Calculus, Pearson, 12th ed., 2010.
Semester 2 · Basic Sciences

Mathematics – II

Code BS 201 MT 4 periods/wk (3T + 1 Tut) SEE 70 · CIE 30

Course objectives

  • Study matrix algebra for linear systems and eigenvalue problems.
  • Provide an overview of ordinary differential equations.
  • Introduce series solutions of differential equations.
  • Cover special functions — Legendre and Bessel.

Course outcomes

  • Solve systems of linear equations and eigenvalue problems.
  • Solve certain first-order and higher-order differential equations.
  • Find series solutions of differential equations.
  • Apply these methods across the curriculum.
UNIT I

Matrices

Elementary row and column operations; rank of a matrix; echelon form; systems of linear equations; linear dependence and independence of vectors; linear and orthogonal transformations; eigenvalues, eigenvectors and their properties; Cayley–Hamilton theorem; quadratic forms; reduction to canonical form by orthogonal transformation; nature of quadratic forms.

UNIT II

ODEs of First Order

Exact first-order differential equations; integrating factors; linear first-order equations; Bernoulli's, Riccati's and Clairaut's differential equations; orthogonal trajectories of a family of curves.

UNIT III

Linear DEs of Higher Order

Linear independence and dependence; solutions of second- and higher-order homogeneous equations with constant coefficients; reduction of order for variable coefficients; non-homogeneous equations; method of variation of parameters; Euler–Cauchy equation; simultaneous linear differential equations.

UNIT IV

Series Solutions of DEs

Ordinary and singular points of an equation; power series solution; series solution about a regular singular point; Frobenius method; Beta, Gamma and error functions.

UNIT V

Special Functions

Legendre's differential equation and polynomials; Rodrigue's formula; generating function and recurrence relations for Pn(x); orthogonal and orthonormal functions; orthogonal property of Pn(x); Bessel's differential equation and functions; derivatives, integrals, recurrence relations and generating function for Jn(x).

Why it matters for BME — Matrices and eigenvalues drive medical image processing, PCA and system modelling; differential equations describe pharmacokinetics, nerve conduction and every dynamic physiological system you'll meet in later years.

Suggested reading

  1. R. K. Jain & S. R. K. Iyengar, Advanced Engineering Mathematics, Narosa, 4th ed., 2014.
  2. B. S. Grewal, Higher Engineering Mathematics, Khanna, 43rd ed., 2014.
  3. M. D. Raisinghania, Ordinary and Partial Differential Equations, S. Chand, 17th ed., 2014.
  4. Erwin Kreyszig, Advanced Engineering Mathematics, Wiley, 9th ed., 2012.
Semester 3 · BME-specific paper

Mathematics – III

Code BS 901 MT 4 periods/wk (3T + 1 Tut) Credits 3 · SEE 70 · CIE 30

Course objectives

  • Introduce functions of complex variables and their properties.
  • Formulate PDEs and solve first-order linear and non-linear PDEs.
  • Study Fourier series and its applications to PDEs.

Course outcomes

  • Determine analyticity and expand functions as Taylor and Laurent series.
  • Evaluate complex and real integrals using the residue theorem.
  • Expand functions as a Fourier series.
  • Solve first- and second-order partial differential equations.
UNIT I

Functions of Complex Variables

Limits and continuity; differentiability and analyticity; necessary and sufficient conditions for analyticity; Cauchy–Riemann equations in polar form; harmonic functions; complex integration; Cauchy's integral theorem and its extension for multiply-connected regions; Cauchy's integral formula; Cauchy's formula for derivatives and applications.

UNIT II

Residue Calculus

Power series; Taylor's series; Laurent's series; zeros and singularities; residues; residue theorem; evaluation of real integrals using the residue theorem; bilinear transformation; conformal mapping.

UNIT III

Fourier Series

Fourier series; Fourier expansions of even and odd functions; convergence of Fourier series; Fourier half-range series.

UNIT IV

Partial Differential Equations

Formation of first- and second-order PDEs; solution of first-order equations; Lagrange's equation; non-linear first-order equations; Charpit's method; higher-order linear equations with constant coefficients.

UNIT V

Fourier Series Applications to PDEs

Classification of linear second-order PDEs; separation of variables (Fourier method); Fourier series solution of one-dimensional heat and wave equations; Laplace's equation.

Why it matters for BME — This is the mathematical heart of biosignals. Fourier series is the gateway to spectral analysis of ECG/EEG, and the heat and wave equations model diffusion, thermal therapy and ultrasound propagation.

Suggested reading

  1. R. K. Jain & S. R. K. Iyengar, Advanced Engineering Mathematics, Narosa, 4th ed., 2014.
  2. B. S. Grewal, Higher Engineering Mathematics, Khanna, 43rd ed., 2014.
  3. Gupta & Kapoor, Fundamentals of Mathematical Statistics, Sultan Chand, 2014.
  4. Erwin Kreyszig, Advanced Engineering Mathematics, Wiley, 9th ed., 2012.
  5. Brown & Churchill, Complex Variables and Applications, McGraw-Hill, 9th ed., 2013.
Semester 4 · Basic Sciences

Mathematics – IV

Code BS 401 MT 4 periods/wk (3T + 1 Tut) Credits 3 · SEE 70 · CIE 30

Course objectives

  • Introduce Laplace, Fourier and Z-transforms and their properties.
  • Introduce numerical methods for standard problem types.
  • Cover curve fitting, correlation and regression.

Course outcomes

  • Solve differential equations using Laplace and Fourier transforms.
  • Solve difference equations using Z-transforms.
  • Find numerical solutions of algebraic, transcendental and ordinary differential equations.
  • Perform regression analysis and interpret the coefficient of correlation.
UNIT I

Laplace Transforms

Introduction; sufficient condition for existence; Laplace transform of derivatives and integrals; translation theorems (first and second shifting); differentiation of the transform (multiplication by t); integration of the transform (division by t); convolution theorem; solving initial value problems.

UNIT II

Fourier Transforms

Introduction; Fourier integrals; Fourier sine and cosine integrals; complex form of the Fourier integral; Fourier transform; Fourier sine and cosine transforms; finite Fourier sine and cosine transforms; properties; convolution theorem for Fourier transforms.

UNIT III

Z-Transforms

Introduction; basic theory; Z-transforms of standard sequences; existence; linearity, translation, scaling; initial and final value theorems; differentiation of the Z-transform; convolution theorem; solution of difference equations using Z-transforms.

UNIT IV

Numerical Methods

Algebraic and transcendental equations — bisection, Newton–Raphson; linear systems — Gauss elimination, Gauss–Seidel iteration; interpolation — Lagrange, Newton's divided difference, Newton's forward and backward; numerical differentiation; numerical solution of ODEs — Taylor's series, Euler, Runge–Kutta 4th order.

UNIT V

Curve Fitting

Curve fitting by least squares; correlation and regression; types of correlation; Karl Pearson's coefficient of correlation; Spearman's rank correlation coefficient; equal ranks; equations to the lines of regression.

Why it matters for BME — Transforms are the backbone of biomedical signal and image processing; Z-transforms drive digital filter design; numerical methods and regression power everything from device calibration to data analysis in clinical studies.

Suggested reading

  1. R. K. Jain & S. R. K. Iyengar, Advanced Engineering Mathematics, Narosa, 4th ed., 2014.
  2. B. S. Grewal, Higher Engineering Mathematics, Khanna, 43rd ed., 2014.
  3. Vasishtha & Gupta, Integral Transforms, Krishna Prakashan, 2014.
  4. Erwin Kreyszig, Advanced Engineering Mathematics, Wiley, 9th ed., 2012.
  5. S. C. Gupta & V. K. Kapoor, Fundamentals of Mathematical Statistics, Sultan Chand, 2014.

Verify: The UCE Mathematics department publishes a single Mathematics-IV paper (BS 401 MT). BME follows this standard fourth mathematics course, but confirm the exact paper against your printed B.E. (BME) IV-Semester Scheme & Syllabus in case the department has issued a branch-specific revision.

02

A comprehensive study plan

Four papers, two years, one habit: steady work beats last-minute cramming. Here's how to pace it — semester by semester, week by week, and unit by unit.

Each paper has five units and runs on a 70-mark end exam + 30-mark internal pattern. A working rule that fits the OU calendar: plan roughly two-and-a-half weeks per unit, keep the last three weeks for full revision and past papers, and never let the tutorial hour go to waste — that's where problem-solving speed is built.

SEMESTER 1 — MATHS I

Build the calculus core

  • Weeks 1–3: Sequences & series — master the convergence tests until you can pick the right one on sight.
  • Weeks 4–6: Differential calculus & curve sketching — mean value theorems, curvature, envelopes.
  • Weeks 7–9: Functions of several variables — partial derivatives, Jacobians, Lagrange multipliers.
  • Weeks 10–12: Multiple integrals & vector calculus — Green's, Gauss's, Stoke's theorems.
  • Final weeks: Mixed problem sets + previous question papers.
SEMESTER 2 — MATHS II

Master matrices & ODEs

  • Weeks 1–3: Matrices — rank, eigenvalues, Cayley–Hamilton, quadratic forms.
  • Weeks 4–6: First-order ODEs — exact equations, integrating factors, Bernoulli/Riccati/Clairaut.
  • Weeks 7–9: Higher-order linear ODEs — variation of parameters, Euler–Cauchy.
  • Weeks 10–12: Series solutions + special functions — Frobenius, Legendre, Bessel.
  • Final weeks: Drill eigenvalue and ODE problems under timed conditions.
SEMESTER 3 — MATHS III

Enter complex analysis & PDEs

  • Weeks 1–3: Complex variables — analyticity, Cauchy–Riemann, Cauchy's theorems.
  • Weeks 4–6: Residue calculus — Taylor/Laurent, residue theorem, conformal mapping.
  • Weeks 7–8: Fourier series — even/odd functions, half-range series.
  • Weeks 9–11: PDEs — Lagrange's equation, Charpit's method.
  • Weeks 12+: Heat & wave equations by separation of variables — the BME-critical unit.
SEMESTER 4 — MATHS IV

Transforms & computation

  • Weeks 1–3: Laplace transforms — shifting theorems, convolution, IVPs.
  • Weeks 4–5: Fourier transforms — sine/cosine, convolution.
  • Weeks 6–7: Z-transforms — difference equations.
  • Weeks 8–10: Numerical methods — Newton–Raphson, Gauss–Seidel, interpolation, RK4.
  • Weeks 11+: Curve fitting, correlation & regression — tie it back to real data.

The weekly rhythm

3×

Theory first, then hands

Read the concept, then work 8–10 problems the same day. Maths is not a spectator subject — the pen has to move.

1×

Own the tutorial hour

The weekly tutorial is where marks are made. Arrive with attempted problems and leave with the doubts cleared.

∞

Weekly recap sheet

Keep one A4 formula-and-method sheet per unit. By exam time you revise from your own five sheets, not the whole book.

Exam & internals strategy

The 30 internal marks are the easiest to secure — attend, submit assignments, and take the mid-terms seriously. That leaves a comfortable cushion for the 70-mark end exam.

Scoring the 70-mark SEE
  • Every unit is examinable — no unit can be safely skipped.
  • Solve the last 5 years of OU question papers; problem patterns repeat even when numbers change.
  • Write full steps — method marks add up even when a final answer slips.
  • Time-box: roughly one mark per minute, and move on when stuck.
Working smart
  • Keep a running "mistakes log" — the same slip repeats until you name it.
  • Group similar problems; learn the type, not the one-off.
  • Use Grewal for worked examples, Kreyszig for depth when a topic won't click.
  • For Maths IV numerical methods, practise the arithmetic by hand — the exam is not open-calculator-heavy.
03

Reference library

Two books cover almost everything across all four papers; the rest are topic specialists worth borrowing when a unit needs more depth.

Higher Engineering Mathematics

B. S. Grewal · Khanna, 43rd ed.

CORE · all four papers · best for worked examples

Advanced Engineering Mathematics

R. K. Jain & S. R. K. Iyengar · Narosa, 4th ed.

CORE · all four papers · prescribed text

Advanced Engineering Mathematics

Erwin Kreyszig · Wiley, 9th ed.

DEPTH · all papers · rigorous explanations

Thomas' Calculus

Thomas, Weir & Hass · Pearson, 12th ed.

MATHS I · calculus foundations

Ordinary & Partial Differential Equations

M. D. Raisinghania · S. Chand, 17th ed.

MATHS II & III · ODEs and PDEs

Complex Variables and Applications

Brown & Churchill · McGraw-Hill, 9th ed.

MATHS III · complex analysis

Integral Transforms

Vasishtha & Gupta · Krishna Prakashan

MATHS IV · Laplace / Fourier / Z

Fundamentals of Mathematical Statistics

S. C. Gupta & V. K. Kapoor · Sultan Chand

MATHS III & IV · statistics, correlation

Sources

Every syllabus on this page is transcribed from the official Osmania University / University College of Engineering PDFs:

  • Mathematics-I & II — Department of Mathematics, UCE(A), OU (common to all branches)
  • Mathematics-III (BME) — BS 901 MT, Department of Mathematics, UCE(A), OU
  • Mathematics-IV — BS 401 MT, Department of Mathematics, UCE(A), OU
  • Course listing — uceou.edu/dept-maths.php · Dept. of Biomedical Engineering

How to use this page

Built as a single self-contained file for hosting on tiiny.host — just upload index.html. It works offline, prints cleanly for a revision handout, and needs no internet except for the web fonts.

Always cross-check unit weightage and any recent revisions against your current printed scheme of instruction, since the department revises the syllabus roughly every four years.

Engineering Mathematics I–IV · BE Biomedical Engineering · Osmania University (UCE-A).  Compiled study reference — not an official university publication.  Prepared 04 July 2026.
